topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was the loudest thing I had ever heard. Dust motes danced in the afternoon sun slicing through the blinds, illuminating the empty chair where Mr. Silas used to sit. I stood by the mahogany desk, my hands gripping the edge so tightly my knuckles were white. He was gone. Retired. The official memo had been polite, sterile, a neat little administrative closure that felt like a deliberate erasure. I replayed the last few weeks in my head—the hurried emails, the casual way he’d dismissed my concerns, the final, almost breezy mention of his impending departure. A knot tightened in my chest, a cold, heavy pressure that made my breath catch. I wanted to apologize, to ask him what I had done wrong, but the words dissolved before they reached my throat. Instead, I just turned, the sound of my own footsteps echoing unnervingly loud in the cavernous space, and walked out into the indifferent afternoon.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in the glass of the elevator doors, scrutinizing the slump of my shoulders, the way my gaze darted everywhere but at the polished steel. Silas hadn't even looked up when he handed me the retirement papers. It was so abrupt, so final, that I hadn't even registered the shock until I was alone. Now, the weight of his absence settled over me like a shroud. I fiddled incessantly with the cuff of my jacket, a nervous habit I hadn't possessed since I was a child. Every time a colleague approached, I would force a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and quickly pivot the conversation to something mundane. The silence he left behind was deafening, amplifying the hollow feeling in my stomach. I felt clumsy, ungrateful, as if I had somehow failed the unspoken contract between us, and I couldn't bear the thought of ever facing him again.

[story 3]

The farewell party was a blur of forced pleasantries and strained laughter. Everyone spoke of Silas’s future endeavors, painting him as a sage embarking on a well-deserved rest. I managed a nod, my smile feeling pasted on, stiff and unnatural. Later, alone in my cramped apartment, the reality of the situation hit me with a dull, sickening thud. He hadn't even stopped to offer a proper goodbye. Just a signed document and a hurried instruction to transfer my files. I paced the length of my living room, the floorboards groaning under my frantic movements. My jaw felt tight, clenched so hard it ached. I picked up a photograph of us from a shared project, tracing the outline of his familiar, kind face with my fingertip. A wave of profound inadequacy washed over me; I felt small, insignificant, a shadow that couldn't even warrant a proper farewell. I sank onto the sofa, pulling my knees to my chest, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.
